narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong indicators of bar passage to maintain high success rates. For context, you're a Black male with an upper-middle-class family background, planning to attend full-time, and your profile includes a Year 1 school grade decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 2, an LSAT score of 34, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.90—several of which stand out when compared to typical applicants. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 academic decile, which ranked you in the bottom 20% of your school that year; this low performance was the strongest signal pushing the prediction toward bar failure, as it suggests challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a 2—well below the average of around 5.7—reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often correlate with bar exam difficulties. Your family's upper-middle income level played a smaller but still notable role, nudging the prediction slightly toward failure, possibly because students from similar brackets sometimes face unique pressures despite the advantages. These three factors together outweighed positives like your full-time enrollment plans, which are common among successful students. Compared to typical students—who average Year 1 and 3 deciles around 5.5-5.7, LSAT scores near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—your lower deciles were particularly glaring red flags, far from the middle-of-the-pack performance the model favors for bar passage. While your LSAT and GPA were somewhat below average too, they weren't the primary drivers here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment to help the school avoid risks. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles, preparing rigorously for a higher LSAT retake, and seeking mentorship tailored for Black students navigating law school pathways—many have turned similar profiles around with targeted improvements. Reapplying after addressing these core academic areas could significantly shift your odds in future cycles.
